themes.name: The Time Machine
themes.tagline: History is stranger, messier, and more fascinating than any textbook ever let on.

items.title: What Did 'Inglourious Basterds' Get Right About WW2?
items.description:
We love it, but can any historical truths be found in Tarantino's epic Inglourious Basterds?? 

War historian Jennifer Howe takes us through the real stories of the SS, Nazi occupation and The Richie boys, portrayed in this movie by Christoph Waltz, Brad Pitt, Diane Kruger, Eli Roth, and Mélanie Laurent.

0:00 Introduction
0:36 Overview of Inglourious Basterds
1:27 Historical context of the 1941 occupied France
2:54 Analysing Christoph Waltz's Hans Landa
4:54 Bureaucracy and systems of the Holoc

items.title: Delphi: Centre of the Ancient World
items.description:
For more than a thousand years, Delphi was considered the centre of the ancient world. Every year a throng of pilgrims climbed the slopes of Mount Parnassus to seek the words of Apollo through the famous Oracle of Delphi.

Today Tristan Hughes is joined by Michael Scott to uncover Delphi's story, the sanctuary that shaped the ancient Mediterranean. How did the Pythia become the most famous oracle of antiquity? Why did rulers travel from across the Greek world to seek its guidance? And what can D

items.title: Get Ready With Me | Working in Henry VIII's Kitchens
items.description:
When we think of Tudor fashion at the court of Henry VIII, we probably imagine the lush gowns and shining doublets that we see in portraits. But what did ordinary, working people wear in the 1500s?  

In this video, Head of Costume Erin Charteris and Historic Kitchens Interpreter Zak Zvinis go through the step by step process of dressing a Tudor Cook to work in Henry VIII’s kitchens at Hampton Court Palace.  

They answer questions like how was the clothing made? Is it practical and comfortable?

items.title: S2 Ep19: Victorian Ballroom Etiquette
items.description:
Gloves or no gloves? How did you get introduced to eligible gentleman? Could he buy you a drink? Today we're reading “Routledge’s Ballroom Companion” from 1866.

________________ 

If you enjoy the podcast and want to support what I’m doing, check out my Patreon! 

https://www.patreon.com/textory

themes.name: Don't Believe the Label
themes.tagline: From vegetable juice to viral family channels to billionaire donors — nothing is quite what it's sold as.

items.title: The Lie Behind V8
items.description:
You think V8 is healthy… but is it really?

Picture this: you’re in the grocery store, determined to make better choices. You skip the soda, avoid sugary sports drinks, and reach for something that feels responsible: V8. Packed with vegetables, it has to be good for you… right?  Well… not so fast.

In this episode of Weird History, we dig into the surprising origins of V8, the truth behind its “healthy” reputation, and the hidden details most people never question. From clever marketing to nutri

Journey through the Blue Ridge Mountains, Shenandoah National Park, Skyline Drive, the Chesapeake Bay, Virginia Beach, and the historic streets of Williamsburg, alongside countless natural and cultural treasures, all captured in stunning ultra-high-definition Travel Video 4K HDR 60fps. Whether you're planning yo

morning_soundtrack.title: The best rock and folk entries in the 2026 Tiny Desk Contest
morning_soundtrack.snippet: The 2026 Tiny Desk contest brought in thousands of entries, including several standouts in the rock and folk genres.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The sound of a campfire you stumbled upon in someone's backyard, surprising and alive.

morning_soundtrack.title: How much should we care about a pop star's baggage?
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Gracie Abrams, one of today's biggest new singer-songwriters, has polarized critics with her confessional writing and A-list upbringing. Why do people care so much that she is taking up space?
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The tension of a dinner table where everyone has an opinion but nobody asked for yours.

morning_soundtrack.title: Melissa Auf Der Maur Announces First New Music In 16 Years, Hole Tease Something For Friday
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Melissa Auf der Maur, who has provided both her musical talents to iconic groups Hole and the Smashing Pumpkins, is releasing her first new music in 16 years. The Montreal-born musician and artist announced BASS WOMB ROOM: My ’90s 4-Track Demos x Field Recordings, which is out September 4 via Envisi
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Unearthed cassette tapes found in a shoebox — lo-fi ghosts of a decade that still won't let go.

morning_soundtrack.title: Pulp Play “Wickerman” For The First Time In 15 Years
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
This past Saturday, Pulp celebrated Rough Trade's 50th Birthday at London’s Royal Festival Hall. They played all the tracks from their latest album More, which came out last year and was their first release on Rough Trade after Pulp signed with the label in 2024.
The post Pulp Play “Wickerman&
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The electric jolt of a song you forgot you loved crashing back into your body like a reunion hug.

morning_soundtrack.title: Steve Lacy, Beck, and More: This Week’s Pitchfork Selects Playlist
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Our weekly playlist highlights songs that our writers, editors, and contributors are listening to on repeat
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: That restless Friday afternoon feeling when your headphones are the only thing standing between you and the week.

morning_soundtrack.title: L7 Bassist Jennifer Finch Dies at 59
morning_soundtrack.snippet: The grunge musician died on Saturday after being diagnosed with an aggressive form of brain cancer
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: A quiet, heavy moment — the kind where you turn the music up louder to mourn someone who made the noise matter.

morning_soundtrack.title: Spin Doctors’ Aaron Comess Nails Avenged Sevenfold’s “Afterlife” As He Hears It for First Time: Watch
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
The alt-rock drummer nailed the song on his first take, following his instincts and meticulous charting.
Spin Doctors’ Aaron Comess Nails Avenged Sevenfold’s “Afterlife” As He Hears It for First Time: Watch
Langdon Hickman
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The rush of watching someone crack a code in real time, instinct and craft colliding in a single take.

morning_soundtrack.title: Soundgarden’s Kim Thayil Sounds Off
morning_soundtrack.snippet: With his memoir A Screaming Life, Soundgarden's legendary guitar innovator Kim Thayil pulls back the curtain on his tight-lipped band.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like peeling back the leather of an old guitar strap to find initials carved underneath — intimate, worn, and overdue.

global_silver_linings.title: After 5-yo Diagnosed with Rare Disease, Little Sister Wanted to Be Her ‘Brave’ Bone Marrow Donor
global_silver_linings.snippet: From Salt Lake City comes the touching story of a 5-year-old girl saved thanks to a bone marrow transplant from none other than her 2-year-old sister. Emma Meline was diagnosed with an extremely rare bone disease after her mother Amanda brought her to the pediatrician with a rash and swelling on her leg. Little Lucy, […] The post After 5-yo Diagnosed with Rare Disease, Little Sister Wanted to Be Her ‘Brave’ Bone Marrow Donor appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: A young child's instinct — when her sister needed saving — was simply to help, reminding us that courage and compassion can live in the smallest of hearts. Stories like this show how love within families can rise to meet even the most frightening medical challenges.

global_silver_linings.title: ‘A place of hope and solutions’: the building promising a better future
global_silver_linings.snippet: A bold new building in the Netherlands promises to  showcase solutions to global problems – and encourage positive action The post ‘A place of hope and solutions’: the building promising a better future appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: Somewhere, people are actively designing spaces dedicated to real-world solutions and collective optimism — proof that hope isn't passive, but something we can literally build. That kind of intentional, grounded thinking is exactly what difficult times call for.

global_silver_linings.title: A flight attendant with 11 years in the air says she’ll never wear shorts on a plane
global_silver_linings.snippet: "The amount of things I have seen on the seat. Wear pants." The post A flight attendant with 11 years in the air says she’ll never wear shorts on a plane appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: A seasoned flight attendant sharing hard-won, practical knowledge is a quiet reminder that everyday expertise — freely given — genuinely keeps people safer. It's the kind of no-fuss, neighbour-helping-neighbour wisdom that never goes out of style.

global_silver_linings.title: In Cuba’s fuel crisis, solar tricycles took the streets
global_silver_linings.snippet: BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Cuba has long been known for its vintage cars — the American ones that kept rolling through decades of embargo and scarcity, repaired with ingenuity and improvised parts. That ingenuity hasn’t gone anywhere. What it’s being directed at now is something else entirely.
global_silver_linings.reason: When fuel ran short in Cuba, communities didn't simply wait — they pedalled forward on solar-powered tricycles, turning scarcity into a cleaner, more human-scaled way of getting around. It's a vivid example of how necessity can nudge us toward solutions that are better for people and the planet alike.

discovery_articles.title: Museo Casa del Mendrugo in Puebla, Mexico
discovery_articles.snippet: La Casa del Mendrugo occupies an old colonial mansion in Puebla. Its name, Mendrugo, refers to the crumbs or charity that the Jesuits received from the parishioners in order to support their ministry. In 2010, an excavation in the building's courtyard uncovered a burial site from more than 2,000 years ago.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: A Jesuit charity house named after breadcrumbs is charming enough — but the real twist is that when workers dug up the courtyard in 2010, they found a burial site two millennia older than the building itself. The mansion was just the newest tenant.

discovery_articles.title: The House of David Bowie in Berlin, Germany
discovery_articles.snippet: The house is nothing special, and the neighborhood even less so. Flanked by a tattoo parlor and a physiotherapy clinic, the entrance to Hauptstraße 155 is an archway made of cast cement stone—a common feature of the Gründerzeit (Wilhelminian era)—featuring a massive oak door decorated with cast-iron floral ornaments. The door lock has two keyholes set at a 90-degree angle to each other, designed for the "pass-through" keys of the early 20th century, which were intended to force the residents of these tenement blocks to lock up.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Bowie wrote 'Heroes' and 'Low' behind that unremarkable oak door in a Schöneberg tenement, which still has its original double-keyholes designed to compel residents to lock up — a small, stubborn piece of early 20th-century distrust preserved in cast iron.

discovery_articles.title: Teddy Bear Park in Stillwater, Minnesota
discovery_articles.snippet: Beloved toys around the world, teddy bears are icons of childhood wonder. These plush effigies put soft paws and friendly faces on one of nature's most dangerous creatures. Normally, they're tiny, confined to bedrooms and car backseats — except in Stillwater.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Stillwater, Minnesota made the quietly radical decision to fill a public park with life-sized teddy bears — a reminder that the thing we gave children to cuddle is, in the wild, an apex predator.

discovery_articles.title: Umtanum Creek Footbridge in Ellensburg, Washington
discovery_articles.snippet: Yakima (pronounced "YAK-uh-maw") Canyon, where the Yakima River traverses a water gap through Manastash Ridge, is an obvious thoroughfare and was used by Native Americans for thousands of years, and then by Euro-American trappers and settlers into the 19th century. It was also an obvious route for a railroad, and the Northern Pacific Railway was built through it in the early 1880s, along the west bank of the river. (The railroad is still in active use and is now part of the BNSF system.) Railroading was an extremely labor-intensive business well into the 20th century, and camps were built along the right-of-way to house the trackwalkers, signal men, section gangs, and other workers.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Long before the Northern Pacific laid its tracks through Yakima Canyon, Native peoples had already mapped the same logic into the land — the river gap was simply too obvious a path for any era to ignore.

discovery_articles.title: The Tower of London Plans to Turn Its Moat Into a ‘Green Classroom’ and Show Off Its Architecture Archives in a Major Renovation
discovery_articles.snippet: The landmark, nearly a millennium old, has housed kings, hosted royal executions and safeguarded Britain's crown jewels. New education centers aim to invite more people to learn about that history
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The Tower of London is turning its ancient moat — once a stinking, strategic barrier — into a green classroom, which feels like exactly the right fate for a ditch that outlived its purpose by about 600 years.

discovery_articles.title: An Unusual-Looking Raccoon Nicknamed Jimothy Is Winning Hearts Across the Internet for His Short, Round Body
discovery_articles.snippet: Jimothy has become a local celebrity in Seattle's Ballard neighborhood and likely has a rare congenital spine condition
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Jimothy's unusually compact, round silhouette likely comes from a rare congenital spine condition — and yet Seattle's Ballard neighborhood has adopted him as a local celebrity, proof that the internet's appetite for a distinguished-looking raccoon is essentially bottomless.

discovery_articles.title: A Man With Paralysis Has Regained Feeling and Movement in His Hands After an Experimental Brain Implant
discovery_articles.snippet: After months of training, the patient can now move his arms and hands and feel the sensation of touch
discovery_articles.ferns_note: After a brain implant and months of patient retraining, a man with paralysis can now feel the sensation of touch again — not just move, but *feel* — which is the detail that quietly changes everything about what this experiment means.

discovery_articles.title: New Women's Pro Baseball League Teams Are Named for These Pioneering American Women, Giving Female Players Another League of Their Own
discovery_articles.snippet: The Firebells, Heights, Queens and Hunters—of San Francisco, New York, Los Angeles and Boston—were inspired by an honorary firefighter, a civil rights activist, a first basewoman and a physician
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The new women's pro baseball league didn't reach for generic team names — each one was shaped around a real, specific woman: an honorary firefighter, a civil rights activist, a first basewoman, a physician, each city's history suddenly legible in a jersey.

discovery_articles.title: Pompeii’s survivors shine in a new documentary series
discovery_articles.snippet: Hosted by actor Tom Hiddleston, Pompeii: Out of Time mixes facts and fiction to bring three real ancient Romans to life.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: We've long told Pompeii's story through its dead, but this documentary series — and Tom Hiddleston, somehow — turns its attention to the survivors, the people who fled the ash and then had to figure out what came next.

discovery_articles.title: Presence, Memory, and Time Merge in the Surreal Works of ‘Architects of Absence’
discovery_articles.snippet: The group exhibition at Gallery Wendi Norris plumbs the dialogue between absence and presence. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: An exhibition built around absence sounds like it risks disappearing into its own concept, but the most interesting tension here is that 'Architects of Absence' insists on presence — of memory, of time, of the people who left the gaps.

featured_read.title: Notes on Complexity: A Buddhist Scientist on the Murmuration of Being
featured_read.snippet: "You are this body, and you are these molecules, and you are these atoms, and you are these quantum entities, and you are the quantum foam, and you are the energetic field of space-time, and, ultimately, you are the fundamental awareness out of which all these emerge."
featured_read.blurb: This essay asks what it actually means to say 'you are made of stardust' — and then keeps pulling the thread until the self dissolves into quantum foam and something stranger takes its place. Settle in for the rare piece of science writing that leaves you feeling both smaller and somehow more whole.

garden_note.note: First light crept over the Zürichberg at 05:13 this morning, catching the lavender in full violet flush and the ripening tomato skins with a warm copper glow — high summer in the Swiss Mittelland is generous, and the garden knows it. Take your coffee outside before the heat builds; there is nowhere better to be right now.
in_season: Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) — harvest stems just as the lower florets open
Outdoor tomatoes — check for signs of first colour on early varieties
Runner beans — pick daily to keep plants productive
Deadhead roses to encourage a late-summer flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: The first-quarter moon sets around midnight, leaving dark skies after for a glimpse of Saturn rising in the southeast — well placed in Aquarius all this week.
garden_note.sun_times: First light arrives at 05:13, sunrise at 05:49, and the long summer evening stretches all the way to sunset at 21:15 with dusk lingering until 21:50.

puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Rebus
puzzle.prompt: What common phrase is suggested by: the word WEATHER written inside the word UNDER, like a letter tucked into an envelope?
puzzle.hint: Think of it spatially: one word is literally contained within the other.

previous_puzzle.label: The Evening Enigma
previous_puzzle.prompt:
Two of these are true. One is a well-dressed lie. Which is it?

1. Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza.
2. A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
3. The shortest war in recorded history lasted approximately six hours, fought between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896.
previous_puzzle.answer: Number 3 is the lie: the Anglo-Zanzibar War lasted between 38 and 45 minutes, not six hours — making it even more remarkably brief than the false version suggests.

news.title: Here’s Your Signature Cake Flavor, Based on Your Birthday
news.blurb: A delightfully silly bit of edible astrology that's worth a click if only to argue with the results.

news.title: Rachel Roddy’s recipe for pasta with courgette, onion and raw tomato salsa | A kitchen in Rome
news.snippet: Hot summer days call for a pasta dish that’s treated like salad leaves – tossed gently through a fresh, room-temperature sauce In her encyclopaedic but not at all stuffy book about Neapolitan food , Jeanne Caròla Francesconi provides half a dozen recipes for pasta with raw tomato sauces suitable for hot days. The one I always notice is vermicelli all’insalata, because of the arrangement of the words. Not the familiar insalata di pasta (pasta salad), but all’insalata (like a salad), which serves as a reminder that, as with salad, the important thing with this family of recipes is that the pasta is treated like leaves of salad and tossed gently but thoroughly with plenty of tasty and suitably cut condiments and dressing.
news.blurb: Rachel Roddy reminds us that the best summer cooking barely qualifies as cooking — and that's precisely the point.

news.title: Hot Wieners
news.snippet: Photo: Murray Hall • Food Styling: Jason Schreiber Spiced meat sauce, onions, mustard, and celery salt are nonnegotiable in this Rhode Island classic. The post Hot Wieners appeared first on Saveur .
news.blurb: A deep dive into Rhode Island's fiercely specific hot wiener tradition, proof that regional food pride is always worth understanding.

recipe.title: Iced Sesame Noodles
recipe.snippet: Iced sesame noodles have become a thing in our house in recent years. Cold noodles plus a creamy sesame sauce and a line up of great toppings equals custom bowls everyone loves! Continue reading Iced Sesame Noodles on 101 Cookbooks
recipe.blurb: These iced sesame noodles are your answer to sweltering evenings — a creamy, cold, endlessly customisable bowl that comes together with very little fuss and disappears even faster.

larder.seasonal_note: Zürich's Tuesday and Saturday markets at Bürkliplatz are heaped with golden Mirabelle plums and the first local Rüebli right now — roast the carrots simply with a little Rapsöl and fresh thyme from your balcony pot, then tuck a handful of those sweet mirabelles alongside for a quietly brilliant summer side.

fern_data.greeting: Pull up a chair — this one's for you, on a morning when the digest spans Victorian ballrooms, the lie inside every V8 can, and a five-year-old who decided her sister's bone marrow was worth fighting for.
